title: Dog Green Nasal Discharge From One Nostril: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticed green mucus from one of your dog's nostrils? Learn about the causes of unilateral discharge and when you should contact your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is it?

Green mucus coming from only one nostril (unilateral discharge) is a sign that your dog is dealing with an active inflammatory or infectious process. While clear discharge is often just allergies, thick green or yellow discharge indicates the presence of white blood cells and potentially bacteria. Because it is only on one side, it suggests the problem is localized—common causes include a foreign object stuck in the nasal passage (like a grass seed), a fungal infection, a tooth root abscess affecting the sinus, or a nasal polyp.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is Medium. While it is typically not a life-threatening emergency that requires a midnight trip to the ER, it is an uncomfortable condition that will not resolve on its own. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice accompanying symptoms like facial swelling, constant sneezing, or if your dog stops eating, you should attempt to be seen as soon as possible.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of your dog’s nose and the discharge can be an invaluable tool for your vet. A photo allows the medical team to see the exact color, thickness, and volume of the mucus before your dog potentially sneezes it away or licks it off in the clinic. Additionally, a photo of your dog's face from the front can help the vet identify subtle facial asymmetry or swelling that might point toward a dental issue or a growth inside the nasal cavity.

faqs.question: Could my dog have something stuck in their nose?
faqs.answer: Yes, unilateral green discharge is a classic sign of a foreign body, such as a blade of grass or a seed, which can cause a localized infection.

faqs.question: Can a bad tooth cause nasal discharge?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The roots of the upper teeth are very close to the nasal passages. An abscessed tooth root can rupture into the sinus, causing green mucus to drain from the nostril on that side.

faqs.question: Should I try to flush my dog's nose at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid putting anything into your dog's nose at home. This can cause the dog to inhale the fluid or push a foreign object deeper into the nasal cavity.
